mnemonics.title: CV History Timeline
mnemonics.content: '1963 ROBERTS SEES SHAPES, 1970 MARR THINKS LAYERS, 1982 SUMMER SCHOOL STARTS, 2012 ALEXNET ACHIEVES ACE' - Remember the key years and pioneers in Computer Vision history.

mnemonics.title: CV Goals
mnemonics.content: RECOGNIZE, LOCALIZE, SEGMENT, UNDERSTAND - The four main goals of Computer Vision in order of complexity.

mnemonics.title: CV Fields
mnemonics.content: AI + ML + MATHS + PHYSICS + NEUROSCIENCE = COMPUTER VISION - The multidisciplinary nature of the field.

acronyms.usage: A deep learning architecture particularly effective for image classification and feature extraction.
The field enabling machines to interpret visual information.

acronyms.expansion: ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge
acronyms.usage: The benchmark competition that drove deep learning adoption in CV.
A selected subset of an image for focused analysis.
Technology used in robotics and autonomous vehicles for navigation.

visualTips.visualization: Picture an upside-down pyramid: at top are raw pixels, above that are edges and textures, then parts of objects, and at the top are complete objects and scenes - showing how CV builds understanding hierarchically.
Visualize a timeline from 1960s (simple shape recognition) to 1980s (feature-based methods) to 2010s (deep learning) - each era building on the previous.
Imagine a digital photo as a grid of numbered boxes (pixels) versus how our eyes see continuous light - the discrete vs continuous nature is key.

analogies.analogy: If you have a dirty photo, Image Processing is like using Photoshop to clean it up, while Computer Vision is like showing the cleaned photo to a person who can then tell you what's in it.
Early CV was like teaching a child to recognize shapes by giving explicit rules. Deep learning is like showing thousands of pictures to a child who learns patterns automatically - much more powerful.
Visual recognition is like teaching a robot to play 'I Spy' - it must identify what it sees and describe it accurately.
Computer Vision is like a bridge builder who needs to know architecture (CS), materials (Physics), how people see (Neuroscience), and how to learn (AI).
